title: ArrayQueue: Build It With Paper
subtitle: See how a circular array queue works by physically moving front and back pointers.
encouragement: Queues are everywhere: print jobs, web server requests, breadth-first search. Once you see the circular trick with your own hands, implementing one in code becomes straightforward.
realWorldExample: Think of a line at a coffee shop. New customers join at the back. The person at the front gets served next. That's a queue: First In, First Out. The circular part? Imagine the line wraps around a round counter so the back of the line can reuse space freed by people who already left.

materials.detail: Draw 6 equally-spaced boxes in a row. Make them big enough to fit small cards inside.
To draw boxes, write index numbers, and label pointers.
Write these letters on them: A, B, C, D, E, F
One marks "front" (where we dequeue from), the other marks "back" (where the next enqueue goes). Label them if you can.

setup: Draw 6 boxes on your paper strip. Number them 0 through 5 underneath.
Place the FRONT paperclip at index 0.
Place the BACK paperclip at index 0.
Both pointers at the same spot means the queue is empty. Lay your letter cards (A-F) nearby.
Remember: FRONT is where we remove from. BACK is where the next item will be placed.

steps.instruction: Let's enqueue the letter A. Pick up the A card.
steps.action: Place A into the box where BACK is pointing (index 0). Then move BACK one step right to index 1.
steps.conceptNote: Enqueue places the item at the back pointer's position, then advances back. The front pointer doesn't move.
steps.hint: BACK always points to the NEXT EMPTY slot, not the last filled slot.

steps.action: Place B at index 1 (where BACK is). Move BACK to index 2.
Place C at index 2. Move BACK to index 3.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [A][B][C][ ][ ][ ]. FRONT at 0, BACK at 3. Three items in the queue.

steps.instruction: Now let's dequeue. Which item gets removed? Look at where FRONT is pointing.
steps.action: Remove the A card from index 0. Move FRONT one step right to index 1.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [ ][B][C][ ][ ][ ]. FRONT at 1, BACK at 3. A is gone.
steps.conceptNote: FIFO: First In, First Out. A was enqueued first, so A is dequeued first. This is the opposite of a stack.
steps.hint: Notice index 0 is now empty. That space will be reused later; that's the circular trick.

steps.action: Place D at index 3 (BACK moves to 4). Place E at index 4 (BACK moves to 5). Place F at index 5 (BACK moves to... where?).
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [ ][B][C][D][E][F]. FRONT at 1, BACK needs to go past index 5.
steps.hint: BACK was at 5 and needs to advance. But there's no index 6. What happens next?

steps.instruction: BACK needs to go past the end. In a circular queue, it wraps around to the beginning. Move BACK to index 0.
steps.action: Move your BACK paperclip to index 0. It loops around!
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [ ][B][C][D][E][F]. FRONT at 1, BACK at 0. The empty slot at index 0 is right where BACK is pointing.
steps.conceptNote: The wrap-around formula is: back = (back + 1) % capacity. With capacity 6: (5 + 1) % 6 = 0. The modulo operator creates the circular behavior.
steps.hint: Imagine bending your paper strip into a ring so index 5 connects to index 0. That's the mental model.

steps.instruction: Dequeue again. Who's at the front?
steps.action: Remove B from index 1. Move FRONT to index 2.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [ ][ ][C][D][E][F]. FRONT at 2, BACK at 0. Two empty slots at indices 0 and 1.

steps.instruction: Now enqueue a new letter. Write G on a spare piece of paper. Where does it go?
steps.action: Place G at index 0 (where BACK is pointing). Move BACK to index 1.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [G][ ][C][D][E][F]. FRONT at 2, BACK at 1. The queue now wraps around!
steps.conceptNote: The queue contents are now: C, D, E, F, G. They span from index 2 through 5 and then wrap to index 0. The data isn't contiguous, but the front/back pointers keep track.
steps.hint: This is why it's called a CIRCULAR queue. The data wraps around the end of the array.

steps.instruction: How many items are in the queue? Count them. Can you figure it out from just the pointers?
steps.action: Count: C, D, E, F, G = 5 items. Formula: (back - front + capacity) % capacity = (1 - 2 + 6) % 6 = 5.
steps.conceptNote: The size formula (back - front + capacity) % capacity handles the wrap-around. When back < front, adding capacity before the modulo gives the correct count.
steps.hint: Without the circular formula, back (1) minus front (2) gives -1, which is wrong. The modulo trick fixes it.

steps.instruction: Dequeue three more times. Say each letter as you remove it.
steps.action: Dequeue C (FRONT moves to 3). Dequeue D (FRONT moves to 4). Dequeue E (FRONT moves to 5).
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [G][ ][ ][ ][ ][F]. Wait, F is still at index 5. FRONT is at 5. Only F and G remain.

steps.instruction: Dequeue F. Where does FRONT go?
steps.action: Remove F from index 5. FRONT wraps around to index 0. (5 + 1) % 6 = 0.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [G][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ]. FRONT at 0, BACK at 1. One item left: G.
steps.conceptNote: FRONT wraps around too! Both pointers use the same modulo logic to circle back to the beginning.

steps.instruction: Dequeue G. What does the queue look like now?
steps.action: Remove G from index 0. Move FRONT to index 1. FRONT and BACK are both at index 1.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [ ][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ]. FRONT = BACK = 1. Queue is empty again.
steps.conceptNote: When FRONT equals BACK, the queue is empty. This is the same condition we started with (both at 0), just at a different position. The circular nature means "empty" can happen at any index, not just 0.
steps.hint: This is why some implementations keep a separate size counter: to distinguish between "completely empty" and "completely full" (both have front == back).

reflection.question: How is a queue different from a stack?
reflection.answer: A stack is LIFO (Last In, First Out), a queue is FIFO (First In, First Out). In a stack, you add and remove from the same end. In a queue, you add at the back and remove from the front.

reflection.question: Why use a circular array instead of shifting all elements forward on each dequeue?
reflection.answer: Shifting every element costs O(n) per dequeue. With a circular array, dequeue just moves the front pointer: O(1). No data moves at all.

reflection.question: When front equals back, is the queue empty or full?
reflection.answer: It could be either! This is an ambiguity in circular queues. Common solutions: keep a size counter, waste one slot (full when back is one behind front), or use a boolean flag.

reflection.question: What's the wrap-around formula for advancing a pointer?
reflection.answer: new_index = (current_index + 1) % capacity. The modulo operator wraps the index back to 0 when it reaches the end of the array.
